title: Ideas for Practicing Participating Participate with awareness of connection to the universe:

pages.text:
59
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
Mindfulness Handout 4c (Mindfulness Worksheets 2–2c, 4–4b)
Ideas for Practicing Participating
Participate with awareness of connection to the universe: 1.		Focus your attention on where your body touches an object (floor or ground, air molecules,
a chair or armrest, your bed sheets and covers, your clothes, etc.). Try to see all the ways
you are connected to and accepted by that object. Consider the function of that object with
relation to you. That is, consider what the object does for you. Consider its kindness in
doing that. Experience the sensation of touching the object, and focus your entire attention
on that kindness until a sense of being connected or loved or cared for arises in your heart.
Examples: Focus your attention on your feet touching the ground. Consider the kindness of
the ground holding you up, providing a path for you to get to other things, not letting you fall
away from everything else. Focus your attention on your body touching the chair you sit in.
Consider how the chair accepts you totally, holds you up, supports your back, and keeps
you from falling down on the floor. Focus your attention on the sheets and covers on your
bed. Consider the touch of the sheets and covers holding you, surrounding and keeping
you warm and comfortable. Consider the walls in the room. They keep out the wind and the
cold and the rain. Think of how the walls are connected to you via the floor and the air in the
room. Experience your connection to the walls that provide you with a secure place to do
things. Go hug a tree. Think of how you and the tree are connected. Life is in you and in the
tree and both of you are warmed by the sun, held by the air and supported by the earth. Try
and experience the tree loving you by providing something to lean on, or by shading you. 2.		Dance to music. 3.		Sing along with music you are listening to. 4.		Sing in the shower. 5.		Sing and dance while watching TV. 6.		Jump out of bed and dance, or sing before getting dressed. 7. 	Go to a church that sings, and join in the singing. 8.		Play karaoke with friends or at a karaoke club or bar. 9.		Throw yourself into what another person is saying.
10.		Go running, focusing only on running.
11. 	Play a sport and throw yourself into playing.
12.		Become the count of the breath, becoming only “one” when you count 1, becoming only
“two” when you count 2, and so on.
13.		Become a word as you slowly say the word over and over and over.
14.		Take a class in improvisational acting.
15.		Take a dance class.
16. Other:

full_text:
Mindfulness Handout 4c (Mindfulness Worksheets 2–2c, 4–4b)
Ideas for Practicing Participating
Participate with awareness of connection to the universe: 1.		Focus your attention on where your body touches an object (floor or ground, air molecules,
a chair or armrest, your bed sheets and covers, your clothes, etc.). Try to see all the ways
you are connected to and accepted by that object. Consider the function of that object with
relation to you. That is, consider what the object does for you. Consider its kindness in
doing that. Experience the sensation of touching the object, and focus your entire attention
on that kindness until a sense of being connected or loved or cared for arises in your heart.
Examples: Focus your attention on your feet touching the ground. Consider the kindness of
the ground holding you up, providing a path for you to get to other things, not letting you fall
away from everything else. Focus your attention on your body touching the chair you sit in.
Consider how the chair accepts you totally, holds you up, supports your back, and keeps
you from falling down on the floor. Focus your attention on the sheets and covers on your
bed. Consider the touch of the sheets and covers holding you, surrounding and keeping
you warm and comfortable. Consider the walls in the room. They keep out the wind and the
cold and the rain. Think of how the walls are connected to you via the floor and the air in the
room. Experience your connection to the walls that provide you with a secure place to do
things. Go hug a tree. Think of how you and the tree are connected. Life is in you and in the
tree and both of you are warmed by the sun, held by the air and supported by the earth. Try
and experience the tree loving you by providing something to lean on, or by shading you. 2.		Dance to music. 3.		Sing along with music you are listening to. 4.		Sing in the shower. 5.		Sing and dance while watching TV. 6.		Jump out of bed and dance, or sing before getting dressed. 7. 	Go to a church that sings, and join in the singing. 8.		Play karaoke with friends or at a karaoke club or bar. 9.		Throw yourself into what another person is saying.
10.		Go running, focusing only on running.
11. 	Play a sport and throw yourself into playing.
12.		Become the count of the breath, becoming only “one” when you count 1, becoming only
“two” when you count 2, and so on.
13.		Become a word as you slowly say the word over and over and over.
14.		Take a class in improvisational acting.
15.		Take a dance class.
16. Other:

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: Ideas for Practicing Participating Participate with awareness of connection to the universe:

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Name one body sensation or one fact.
Choose one kind next step or stop for rest.

routine_integration.start_the_day_use: eligible as a rotating daily skill, never more than one DBT/ADHD skill in an hour block
routine_integration.recommended_block: after body check or before decompression
